Policy details

pages.chapterTitle: Life Insurance and the People Who Depend on You
Life Insurance and the People Who Depend on You

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: > A person’s need for life insurance is determined by the financial responsibilities their life supports—not only by the salary they earn.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Choose one existing life-insurance policy and find its schedule.
Record:

pages.chapterTitle: Life Insurance and the People Who Depend on You

blocks.text: 1. Whose life is insured 2. The cover amount 3. The policy term 4. The premium due date 5. The nominee 6. Where the full document is stored
Then ask:
*“What responsibility was this policy meant to protect?”*

If no one can answer, mark the policy for a proper review. In the next chapter, we will look beyond hospital bills and death to the risks families often overlook: accidents, disability, critical illness, property loss and the benefits already available through an employer.

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance
pages.epigraph: “A family may survive the loss of an asset more easily than the long-term loss of a person’s ability to work or provide care.”

blocks.text: The Hospital Bill Was Paid. The Income Loss Was Not.
A working mother was involved in a serious road accident. Her health insurance paid much of the eligible hospital bill, but recovery took several months. During that time:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

items: Her husband reduced his working hours

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: The family had believed health insurance would protect them from the financial impact of an accident. It protected them from part of the medical cost. It did not replace lost income, pay for every rehabilitation expense or manage the changes required at home. Protection must therefore be examined in layers. Health insurance is one layer. Life insurance is another. But disability, accidents, serious illness, property loss and temporary income interruption may require different forms of preparation.
Begin With the Risk, Not the Product

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Before buying another policy, ask:
**“What financial event are we trying to protect ourselves against?”**
A family may want protection against:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

items: Death of a homemaker or caregiver

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: No single policy necessarily covers all these risks.
Personal-Accident Insurance
Personal-accident insurance provides defined benefits when an eligible injury or death results from an accident, subject to the policy terms. Benefits may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Not every policy includes every benefit.
Accidental Death Is Not the Same as Life Cover

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: A personal-accident policy pays only when the insured event meets the policy’s definition of an accident. Ordinary life insurance may cover death from a much wider range of causes, subject to its own terms. For example:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance
Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: This simplified table is educational. The policy wording decides the actual benefit. Accident cover should not automatically replace life insurance.
Permanent Total Disability
A policy may define permanent total disability through specified losses or an inability to perform work. Definitions differ. A benefit may depend on:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Do not assume that an inability to perform your present job automatically qualifies. Read the definition carefully.
Permanent Partial Disability

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: A permanent injury may reduce function without causing total disability. Policies may pay a stated percentage of the insured benefit for specified losses. For example, a policy may contain a table assigning different percentages to different injuries. The policy—not an informal estimate—determines the payable amount.
Temporary Total Disability
Some policies may pay a weekly benefit when an accident temporarily prevents the insured person from working, subject to:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: For a freelancer, business owner or homemaker, eligibility and documentation may differ. Confirm how the insurer establishes financial loss.
Why Homemakers Need Accident Protection Too
A homemaker may not lose a salary after an accident, but the household may incur:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

items: Reduced working hours for another adult

blocks.text: Her ability to provide care has measurable financial value. Do not evaluate accident protection only through salary.
Critical-Illness Cover

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: A critical-illness policy generally pays a defined lump sum after the diagnosis of a listed condition that meets the policy’s definition and other requirements. It is usually a benefit policy , not ordinary reimbursement of the hospital bill. The payment may be used for:

items: Treatment not fully covered by health insurance

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Diagnosis Alone May Not Be Enough
A condition must meet the policy’s exact definition. The policy may specify:
A familiar disease name does not guarantee that every diagnosis of that disease is covered.

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Critical Illness and Health Insurance Perform Different Jobs

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

rows: Pays a defined benefit after an eligible diagnosis
Claim generally relates to actual treatment cost
Benefit may not depend on the exact hospital bill
Can cover many illnesses and accidents
Covers only listed conditions meeting definitions

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

rows: May support repeated claims within limits
May terminate or reduce after a qualifying claim
Can support treatment, income and household needs

blocks.text: One is not automatically a substitute for the other.
Disability and Income Protection

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Long-term disability can be more financially disruptive than a short hospital stay. A family may face:
Protection may come from several places:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: India does not offer one simple, universal private income-protection structure for every worker. Available products and definitions vary. Focus on identifying the gap rather than assuming one policy will replace income completely.

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Build a Disability Scenario
Ask:

1. If I could not perform my work for six months, what income would stop? 2. Which expenses would continue? 3. What new care costs might arise? 4. Would another adult reduce work? 5. What insurance or employer benefit would apply? 6. How long would emergency savings last? 7. Which loans would remain? 8. Is the home physically accessible? 9. Who would manage children or dependants? 10. What professional support might be needed?
This exercise is not meant to frighten you. It reveals whether a serious gap exists.

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Employer Protection Benefits
An employer may provide:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance
Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Do not assume every benefit applies automatically. Ask the employer or human-resources team for official documentation.
Your Employer-Benefit Inventory

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Important questions include:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

items: Does cover continue during unpaid leave?
Does the employee nominate beneficiaries separately?
Is the benefit based on salary?
What documents would the family need?
Is there a deadline for making a claim?
Can the benefit be continued personally?

blocks.text: Protection During a Career Break

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: A mother may lose or reduce several benefits when she leaves employment. These may include:
Before leaving a job, review:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: 1. Which benefits end immediately? 2. Which continue until a later date? 3. Can health cover be migrated or ported? 4. Should personal cover be arranged before leaving? 5. What happens to provident-fund and retirement accounts? 6. Are nominations current? 7. Is any final benefit payable? 8. What documents should be downloaded?
Do not wait until after the last working day to ask.
Home Insurance
A home may face financial loss from events such as:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: A policy may protect:
The building
The physical structure, subject to ownership and policy terms.
Contents
Furniture, appliances, clothing and other eligible household possessions.
Valuables

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Jewellery, art or other high-value items may require separate declaration, limits or special cover.
Liability

Some policies may include liability protection for specified harm to other people or property. Coverage depends on the policy. Natural-catastrophe, theft and accidental-damage terms can vary significantly.
Market Value and Rebuilding Cost
The market price of a home includes factors such as:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: The cost of rebuilding the structure is a different calculation. A building policy may use reconstruction cost rather than the full market value of the property. Confirm:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: If You Live in a Rented Home
The landlord may insure the building. That does not necessarily protect your belongings. A tenant may still want to consider protection for:
Check the rental agreement and policy terms.
Housing-Society or Lender Insurance

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: A housing society or lender may arrange some insurance, but do not assume it protects everything. Ask:

items: Does it cover only the structure?
Can you obtain the policy document?
Does cover continue after the loan ends?

blocks.text: Vehicle Insurance

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Vehicle insurance may include different protections, such as:
Third-party cover and own-damage cover serve different purposes. Before renewal, check:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

items: Insured declared value or applicable valuation

blocks.text: Do not select a policy solely because it is the cheapest option displayed.
Business and Professional Protection
A mother earning from home or running a small business may need to consider:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance
Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: The need depends on the nature and size of the work. A tutor using a laptop has different risks from someone producing packaged food or operating a salon. Insurance does not replace legal, safety, licensing or quality responsibilities.
Professional Indemnity
Professional-indemnity insurance may protect against specified claims arising from professional errors, omissions or negligence. It may be relevant to certain:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Coverage depends heavily on the profession and policy wording. It does not protect dishonesty, intentional misconduct or every dissatisfied customer.
Product Liability

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: A business selling physical products may face claims if a product causes injury or damage. Product-liability protection may be relevant for businesses involving:
Proper testing, labelling, licensing and quality control remain essential.
Cyber Protection
Cyber insurance may cover certain costs connected with:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: A household or small business should still maintain basic digital safety:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Insurance cannot make unsafe digital practices harmless.
Government and Statutory Protections

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Depending on employment, income, occupation and eligibility, a person may have access to statutory or government-supported benefits. These can change and may include protection connected with:
Check eligibility through official government portals or authorised institutions. Do not pay unofficial agents merely to “unlock” a benefit.

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: Do Not Insure Every Small Expense
Insurance works best for losses that would be financially difficult to absorb. Insuring every minor inconvenience may:
A useful principle is:
**Use savings for manageable losses and insurance for losses that could seriously damage the family’s finances.**

Example: One Family, Several Layers
The Rao family has:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

items: Term cover on both earning adults

blocks.text: Their remaining concerns include:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

items: The mother’s accident cover has a low disability benefit.
Employer life cover will end if either adult changes jobs.
Important benefits are not recorded in one place.
The father’s small consulting business stores customer data but has no cyber procedure.
No one has checked whether the family’s rented-home contents are adequately protected.

blocks.text: The solution may not be five new policies. Their first steps could be:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: 1. Review disability protection. 2. Record employer-benefit details. 3. Strengthen data security. 4. Verify home-contents cover. 5. Decide which residual risks require insurance.
Protection planning begins with gaps, not shopping.
How to Compare Protection Products
For each proposed policy, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.text: 1. What exact event triggers payment? 2. Is it reimbursement or a fixed benefit? 3. How much is paid? 4. How is disability or illness defined? 5. What waiting or survival period applies? 6. What exclusions apply? 7. Does the benefit reduce after a claim? 8. Does another policy already cover the same risk? 9. Does the policy end at a particular age? 10. Can the premium change? 11. How is a claim made? 12. What evidence will be required?
If the trigger cannot be explained clearly, do not buy yet.
Common Mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

items: Assuming health insurance covers income loss
Treating accident cover as a substitute for life insurance
Buying critical-illness cover without reading disease definitions
Counting employer benefits without confirming them
Forgetting that workplace cover may end after resignation

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

items: Assuming a lender’s policy protects the whole family
Believing housing-society insurance covers personal possessions
Insuring the market value of land as building reconstruction cost
Buying add-ons without understanding their trigger

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

items: Duplicating several small policies while leaving major gaps
Insuring minor expenses but not catastrophic ones
Buying business insurance without meeting basic legal and safety requirements
Failing to tell the family what protection exists
Never updating cover after a career or family change

pages.chapterTitle: Protection Beyond Basic Insurance

blocks.label: RED FLAG: BE CAREFUL IF…
blocks.text: A policy is described as covering “every accident” or “every illness.” Disability definitions are not shown to you. A salesperson guarantees a critical-illness claim. Employer benefits are quoted without official documentation. You are pressured to buy several riders together. The proposed cover duplicates something you already have. Building cover is based on a figure no one can explain. The insurer or intermediary cannot be independently verified. Premiums must be paid to a personal account. You are asked to conceal your occupation or medical history. A caller requests an OTP, PIN or banking password. A business policy is sold without asking what your business actually does. Exclusions are dismissed as “standard and unimportant.” You are told that safety procedures are unnecessary because insurance exists.

In the next chapter, we will organise the financial information every mother should know—so that accounts, policies, loans and documents can be found when the family needs them.

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know
pages.epigraph: “Financial information is useful only when the right person can find, understand and act on it.”

blocks.text: Everything Was Carefully Stored—On One Person’s Phone

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

blocks.text: A family appeared financially organised. Their bills were paid automatically. Policies were renewed online. Investments were tracked through mobile applications. Important documents were stored digitally. Then the person who managed everything was unexpectedly hospitalised. The family soon discovered that:

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

items: Only he knew which accounts existed
Policy documents were buried in his email
Important contacts were saved only on his phone
No one knew which payments were automatic
Several passwords could not be recovered easily
The home-loan statement was stored in an unfamiliar application
The family knew there were investments but not where they were held

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

blocks.text: Nothing had been deliberately hidden. The system simply depended on one person being constantly available. That is not a secure financial system. A family does not need to share every password or combine every account. But essential information should never disappear when one person is unavailable.
Financial Awareness Is Not the Same as Financial Control
You do not need to manage every transaction personally. You should, however, understand:

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

items: Whom to contact during an emergency
What legal access arrangements are in place

blocks.text: Shared awareness protects the entire family.

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

blocks.text: Build a Family Financial Information System
A reliable system has four qualities.
1. Complete
It includes every important financial category.
2. Current
It is reviewed and updated regularly.
3. Secure
Sensitive information is protected from unauthorised access.
4. Accessible

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

blocks.text: The appropriate trusted person can locate it when genuinely needed. Accessibility does not mean keeping every password in an open notebook. Security and preparedness must work together.
What Your Family Financial File Should Contain
Your file may be physical, digital or a carefully organised combination. Include summaries of:

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

blocks.text: 1. Personal identification 2. Bank accounts 3. Investments 4. Retirement accounts 5. Loans and credit 6. Insurance 7. Property and major assets 8. Income and business interests 9. Regular financial commitments 10. Tax records 11. Nomination and succession documents 12. Trusted professional contacts 13. Digital accounts and recovery instructions 14. Emergency actions
The file should tell the family where information is held , not necessarily expose every confidential detail.
Section 1 — Personal Identification

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

blocks.text: Record the location—not unsecured copies—of documents such as:

items: Disability or medical certificates, if relevant

blocks.text: Check:

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

items: Mobile numbers and email addresses are controlled by the correct person

blocks.text: Do not send identity documents casually through messaging applications. Where copies must be shared, use masking or watermarking where appropriate and permitted.
Section 2 — Bank Accounts
For each bank account, record:

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

items: Last four digits of the account number
Location of cheque books or passbooks, if relevant

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

Bank-Account Inventory

items: Keep them active for a clear purpose
Close them through the proper process

Modern financial lives depend heavily on:
The challenge is to preserve access without weakening security.

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

blocks.text: Do Not Create an Unsafe “Master Password Sheet”
Never keep an unprotected list containing:

A safer approach may include:

items: A trusted person who knows that the system exists

It does not need to contain the passwords themselves.
Mobile Numbers Matter
Many financial accounts depend on a registered mobile number. Review:

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

items: Whose name the mobile connection is in
What happens after death or incapacity
Whether a replacement SIM could be obtained through proper procedures
Which accounts use the number for authentication
Whether the family can contact the institution without accessing the phone

When One Partner Resists the Conversation
A defensive response may come from:

items: Belief that finance is one person’s responsibility
A wish to protect the other person from worry

blocks.text: Begin gently:

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

Focus first on:
If financial information is deliberately withheld in a way that affects your rights or safety, seek appropriate independent support.

pages.chapterTitle: The Financial Information Every Mother Should Know

items: Keeping important details only on one phone
Sharing every password instead of creating proper access arrangements
Writing PINs and OTP instructions in an unsecured notebook
Failing to record ownership and nomination
Storing originals and all backups together

items: Allowing registered mobile numbers to become inactive
Using one weak password across financial accounts
Leaving outdated identity copies in messaging applications
Recording asset values but not document locations
Creating a file once and never updating it
Making the system so complicated that no one uses it

In the next chapter, we will clarify nominations, wills and succession—three areas families often postpone until it is too late to ask questions.

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple
pages.epigraph: “Good succession planning does not begin with fear. It begins with making sure the people you love are not left with avoidable confusion.”

blocks.text: The Account Had a Nominee—but the Family Still Disagreed

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: After a woman died, her family believed one bank account would be easy to settle because she had registered a nominee. The bank processed the claim after completing its requirements. But a disagreement followed. Some family members believed the nominee automatically owned the money. Others believed it belonged to the legal heirs. No one knew whether the deceased woman had made a will. The nomination helped the bank identify whom it could pay through its settlement process. It did not answer every question about the final inheritance. This distinction is one of the most important lessons in succession planning:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: **Nomination can simplify administration, but it does not necessarily replace inheritance law or a valid will.**
The legal result can depend on:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: This chapter will help you organise the questions. It cannot determine who will legally inherit in an individual case. Obtain advice from a qualified Indian succession lawyer when preparing or reviewing your plan.
What Is Succession Planning?
Succession planning is the process of deciding and documenting what should happen to your financial and legal affairs after your death. It may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Telling trusted people where instructions can be found

blocks.text: A succession plan is not only for wealthy families. Even a modest estate may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: The Difference Between a Nominee and a Beneficiary
These words are often used as if they mean the same thing. They may not.

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Nominee
A nominee is a person recorded with an institution to receive, claim or administer an asset after the holder’s death according to the rules governing that asset.
Beneficiary or legal heir

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: A beneficiary is a person entitled under a valid will. A legal heir is a person entitled under applicable succession law when those rules apply. Depending on the asset and law, a nominee may receive an asset on behalf of the person or people ultimately entitled to it. For bank deposits, RBI guidance explains that payment to a valid nominee can discharge the bank’s liability while not necessarily defeating claims that legal heirs may have against the nominee. RBI guidance on deceased depositors (https://www.rbi.org.in/Scripts/NotificationUser.aspx?Id=9862)

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Because asset categories can follow different legal rules, avoid making one general assumption about every nomination.
What Nomination Does Well
A valid and current nomination may help:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Identify a person who can approach the institution
Prevent an asset from remaining unclaimed
Provide the institution with clear records
Help manage an asset while succession is completed

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: SEBI describes nomination as a facility through which an investor identifies a person who can claim securities or applicable mutual-fund proceeds after the investor’s death. Nomination procedures for securities and mutual funds have also changed over time, so investors should check current requirements with the regulated institution. SEBI investor guidance on nomination (https://investor.sebi.gov.in/market-nomination.html)
What Nomination May Not Do
Nomination may not:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Override succession law in every situation
Cover assets for which no nomination was made
Remove the need for legal documentation
Give the nominee unlimited personal ownership

blocks.text: Treat nomination as one part of the plan—not the entire plan.

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Review Nomination by Asset
Nomination rules can differ across:
For each asset, verify through the institution’s official process:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: 1. Is nomination available? 2. Who is currently nominated? 3. Can more than one nominee be named? 4. Can a minor be nominated? 5. Who may receive on behalf of a minor? 6. Does a joint holding change how nomination works? 7. What happens after one joint holder dies? 8. What documents will be required? 9. How can nomination be changed or cancelled? 10. Has the institution acknowledged the nomination?
Do not rely on a handwritten note that was never registered with the institution.
Joint Ownership Is Not One Simple Arrangement

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: A joint account or asset may use operating instructions such as:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: These instructions may affect who can operate the account and how the institution processes it after a death. They do not necessarily determine final inheritance. For bank deposits, RBI guidance distinguishes accounts with nomination or survivorship clauses from those without them and explains separate settlement procedures. RBI guidance on settlement of deceased deposit accounts (https://www.rbi.org.in/Scripts/NotificationUser.aspx?Id=9862) For every joint asset, record:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Do not assume that adding someone as a joint holder is always a safe substitute for estate planning. It can create present ownership, tax, control and family consequences.
What Is a Will?

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: A will is a legal declaration of how a person wants their property to be dealt with after death. The person making the will is commonly called the testator . A will may:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Explain how debts and expenses should be handled

blocks.text: The will takes effect after death. While legally competent, the testator may generally alter or revoke it according to applicable law.

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Who Is an Executor?
An executor is the person appointed to administer the estate according to the will and the law. The role may involve:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Choose someone who is:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Consider naming an alternate executor. Do not appoint someone without discussing the responsibility with them.
Basic Will-Execution Requirements

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: The legal requirements depend on the applicable law and personal circumstances. For many ordinary wills governed by Section 63 of the Indian Succession Act, 1925, the testator must sign or mark the will—or direct another person to sign in the testator’s presence—and the document must be attested by at least two witnesses in the manner specified by law. Indian Succession Act, 1925—Section 63 (https://www.indiacode.nic.in/handle/123456789/2385?locale=en) Do not use that sentence as a do-it-yourself legal checklist. Questions may arise about:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Property in more than one jurisdiction

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Use a qualified lawyer when the estate, family or ownership structure is more than very simple.
Witnesses Matter
A witness may later be required to confirm how the will was executed. As a practical precaution, witnesses should generally be:

items: Not beneficiaries or closely connected to a benefit under the will

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: The legal consequences of a beneficiary or related person acting as a witness can vary. Obtain legal advice rather than improvising. Record:
Do not ask witnesses to sign a document they have not seen the testator sign or acknowledge in the legally appropriate manner.
Registration and Notarisation

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Families often believe a will must be notarised or registered to be valid. The correct legal position can depend on the type of document and circumstances. Registration may provide useful evidence and custody advantages, but it does not cure poor drafting, incapacity, coercion or incorrect execution. Similarly, notarisation does not replace legal execution requirements. Ask a lawyer:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Would registration help in the relevant state?
Where should the original be stored?
How will later alterations or replacement be handled?
What evidence of capacity should be preserved?
Are additional steps appropriate because of age, health or family conflict?

blocks.text: What Happens Without a Will?

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Dying without a valid will is called dying intestate . The estate is then distributed according to the succession law applicable to the person and property. The result may depend on:
Do not assume:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Sons and daughters are treated differently in every situation
A married daughter has no rights
Verbal family wishes will be followed
One family member can decide for everyone

blocks.text: Succession rights are a legal question, not a family custom.
A Will Does Not Control Every Asset in the Same Way

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Some assets may pass or be administered through:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: The will should be coordinated with these arrangements. For example, if a will names one person but an account has a different nominee, the mismatch may create confusion even when the legal outcome can eventually be determined. Create one consistent estate map.
Assets Commonly Forgotten
A will or estate inventory may overlook:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple
Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Personal loans payable by the deceased

blocks.text: The will need not necessarily list every account number in its main text. A separate, securely updated asset inventory can help the executor locate property without requiring a new will after every small financial change. Ask a lawyer how best to coordinate the inventory with the will.
The Residuary Clause

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: After specific gifts and expenses, some property may remain. This is called the residue of the estate. A residuary clause states who should receive that remaining property. Without a suitable clause, omitted or later-acquired assets may create partial intestacy or other complications. This is one reason copying a short online will format can be risky.
Planning for Minor Children
A will can record wishes concerning guardianship and property for minor children, but the final legal process may involve the court and the child’s welfare. Consider:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: A child with additional or lifelong needs

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: A guardian for the child and a person managing the child’s money need not always be the same person. Obtain legal advice to structure the arrangement properly.
Do Not Leave Large Assets Directly to a Minor Without Advice
A minor may not be able to receive or manage assets in the same way as an adult. A poorly designed gift can create:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: A lawyer may discuss appropriate structures, depending on the family’s needs and the law.
Children With Additional Needs
A child who may require lifelong support needs specialised planning. Consider:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Do not leave this planning to a general will template. Consult professionals experienced in disability and succession law.
A Woman’s Own Will Matters
A mother may own:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Do not assume her property will automatically pass exactly as she wishes. She should understand:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Whether inherited or gifted property has documentation
How minor children or dependants should be protected

blocks.text: Succession planning is not only the husband’s responsibility.
Jewellery and Family Expectations

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Jewellery often creates disagreement because legal ownership, emotional expectations and family custom may not match. Record where appropriate:

items: Who received it as a gift

blocks.text: Do not assume that jewellery worn by one person is legally owned by another family member. Obtain advice where ownership may be disputed.

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Property Ownership
For each property, verify:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple
Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Property succession can be complicated. Do not rely on informal family assurances.
Business Succession
A family business may depend on one person’s:
A will alone may not keep the business operating. The plan may also need:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Coordinate business succession with legal and financial professionals.
Power of Attorney Is Not a Will

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: A power of attorney authorises another person to act during the maker’s lifetime according to its terms. It generally does not replace a will and usually does not continue as authority after the maker’s death. A will operates after death. Do not confuse:
Each performs a different legal function.
A Will Cannot Replace Incapacity Planning

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: A will does not help the family manage finances while the person is alive but unable to act. Incapacity planning may involve:
The available tools and formalities can vary. Obtain legal advice.

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Debts Do Not Simply Disappear
An estate may have to address valid liabilities before beneficiaries receive property. The executor may need to identify:
Beneficiaries should not distribute or sell estate property casually before obtaining appropriate legal guidance.

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Store the Will Properly
The original will should be:

items: Accessible to the executor after death
Easy to identify as the current version

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Possible arrangements may include secure personal custody, professional custody, registration where chosen, or another legally appropriate system. The executor should know where the original is held. Do not place the only original somewhere no one can access or identify.
Avoid Several Conflicting Originals
Repeatedly signing informal versions can create uncertainty over which will is current. When updating:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Tell the executor where the new original is stored

blocks.text: Do not write casual amendments in the margins.
When to Review a Will
Review after:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Death or incapacity of an executor

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Creation or closure of a trust

blocks.text: A review does not always require a new will. A lawyer can advise.
The Succession Conversation
A practical family conversation can cover:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Where the asset inventory is kept
Which personal wishes should be respected

blocks.text: The conversation does not need to disclose every inheritance amount. Its purpose is to prevent complete confusion.

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Example: Coordinating the Pieces
Anita owns:

items: A bank account with her sister as nominee
Mutual funds with her husband as nominee
A flat held jointly with her husband
A will made before her second child was born

blocks.text: Her plan needs review because:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: The will may no longer reflect her family.
Her business is absent from the old will.
Jewellery ownership and intended recipients are not documented.
The joint property arrangement needs legal review.
Nominations and the will may not be coordinated.
Guardianship wishes do not address the second child.

blocks.text: Updating only one nomination would not solve the entire problem. She needs one coordinated legal plan.

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Common Mistakes

items: Believing a nominee always becomes the final owner
Treating nomination as a substitute for a will
Assuming joint ownership resolves succession automatically
Copying a will from the internet without legal review
Using beneficiaries as witnesses without advice
Failing to appoint an alternate executor
Leaving large assets directly to a minor without planning

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Keeping the will where no one can find it
Assuming power of attorney continues after death

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: Never reviewing the will after a major life event
Leaving family custom to replace legal documentation
Treating personal law as identical for every Indian family

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.label: RED FLAG: SEEK LEGAL ADVICE PROMPTLY IF…
blocks.text: Family members disagree about ownership. A person may be signing under pressure. The testator has a condition affecting memory or decision-making. A beneficiary is arranging the will and excluding others from the process. Property is located in more than one country. The family includes children from different relationships. A dependant may need lifelong support. A business or partnership is involved. Ancestral or inherited property is disputed. Major assets are held through joint ownership. The family expects a challenge to the will. Original documents are missing. A nominee and will appear inconsistent. The proposed executor has a conflict of interest. A person is asked to sign a will they cannot read or understand. A legal professional is not independently chosen by the testator.

Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.label: REMEMBER THIS
blocks.text: > A nomination helps an institution process an asset. A will and the applicable law help determine how an estate should ultimately be administered and inherited. A secure plan coordinates all three.

blocks.label: TRY THIS TODAY
blocks.text: Choose one bank account, insurance policy or investment.
Verify through the institution’s official records:

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: 1. Whether a nomination is registered 2. The nominee’s name 3. Whether the nominee is still appropriate 4. Whether the institution has current contact information 5. Where the nomination acknowledgement is stored

Do not change a complex nomination until you understand how it interacts with your will and succession plan. This completes Part II. In Part III, we will begin building long-term wealth—starting with the two forces that quietly shape every future goal: inflation and compounding.
Official references used for this chapter

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

items: RBI guidance on settlement of claims for deceased depositors (https://www.rbi.org.in/Scripts/NotificationUser.aspx?Id=9862)

blocks.text: Laws, regulations and institutional procedures change. Readers should verify current requirements and obtain advice suited to their personal law, assets, family structure and jurisdiction.
Growing Long-Term Wealth

pages.chapterTitle: Nominations, Wills and Succession Made Simple

blocks.text: Protection keeps a difficult event from destroying your plan. Long-term investing helps your plan keep pace with the future.
PART III
Growing Long-Term Wealth
A practical step toward lasting financial security.

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting
pages.epigraph: “Money does not need to remain the same number. It needs to retain the ability to support the life you are planning.”

blocks.text: The Goal Had Not Changed—but Its Price Had

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: When a mother’s daughter was five, she estimated that a particular course might one day cost ₹10 lakh. She began saving, but she continued to think of ₹10 lakh as the final goal. Twelve years later, the same type of course cost far more. She had saved carefully. The problem was not a lack of discipline. She had planned for today’s price instead of tomorrow’s cost. That is what inflation does. It quietly changes how much money a future goal may require. Compounding can work in the opposite direction. It allows invested money—and the growth it earns—to generate further growth over time.

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: One force makes future goals more expensive. The other can help your money prepare for them. Understanding both is essential for long-term security.
What Is Inflation?

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Inflation is the general rise in prices over time. When prices rise, the same amount of money buys less than it did before. This reduction is called a loss of purchasing power. If household expenses are ₹60,000 a month today and the general cost of those expenses rises, ₹60,000 may no longer support the same lifestyle in the future. Inflation does not affect every expense equally. Prices may rise differently for:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: A general inflation figure is useful for understanding the economy, but your personal inflation rate depends on what your family actually buys.
A Simple Illustration

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Suppose an expense costs ₹1,00,000 today and rises by 6% in one year. Future cost: ₹1,00,000 × 1.06 = ₹1,06,000 If it rises by another 6% the following year: ₹1,06,000 × 1.06 = ₹1,12,360 The second year’s increase is calculated on the new amount, not the original ₹1,00,000. That is compounding—working against you through rising costs.
How Future Cost Is Estimated
A commonly used formula is:
**Future cost = Present cost × (1 + inflation rate)ⁿ**
Where n is the number of years. Suppose:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Estimated future cost: ₹10,00,000 × (1.07)¹² ≈ ₹22,52,000 This is an estimate, not a guarantee. The actual cost may be higher or lower. The calculation helps you avoid planning for a future goal using only today’s price.
How Long It May Take Prices to Double

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: A quick mental estimate is the Rule of 72 . Divide 72 by the assumed annual rate. At 6% inflation: 72 ÷ 6 = approximately 12 years At 8% inflation: 72 ÷ 8 = approximately 9 years This is only a shortcut. It is not exact, and real inflation changes over time. But it reminds us that a goal many years away may cost dramatically more than it does today.
Your Personal Inflation May Be Higher

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Suppose a retired couple spends heavily on healthcare and domestic assistance. Even if general inflation is moderate, their personal cost of living may rise faster because the expenses they rely on are increasing more quickly. A family with school-age children may experience strong education inflation. A renter may face changing housing costs. A long-term plan should therefore use thoughtful assumptions for individual goals rather than one number for everything.
Why Cash Alone May Not Build Long-Term Security
Cash and savings accounts are useful for:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: But money intended for goals many years away may lose purchasing power if its after-tax growth remains below inflation. Suppose:

items: Tax reduces the effective return further

blocks.text: The account balance grows, but its ability to purchase the future goal may decline. This does not make cash bad. It means cash has a different job from a long-term investment.

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Nominal Return and Real Return
Nominal return
The percentage growth you see before adjusting for inflation.
Real return
The approximate growth in purchasing power after inflation. A simplified estimate is:
**Real return ≈ Nominal return − inflation**
For example: Nominal return: 8% Inflation: 6% Approximate real return: 2% A more precise calculation is:
**Real return = [(1 + nominal return) ÷ (1 + inflation)] − 1**

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Using the same assumptions: (1.08 ÷ 1.06) − 1 ≈ 1.89% Tax and costs may reduce the investor’s final real return further.
What Is Compounding?

Compounding occurs when growth is earned not only on the original amount but also on earlier growth. Suppose ₹1,00,000 earns 8% annually. After one year: ₹1,00,000 × 1.08 = ₹1,08,000 After two years: ₹1,08,000 × 1.08 = ₹1,16,640 The second year’s ₹8,640 growth includes:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Over long periods, this repeated process can become powerful.
Time Changes the Result
Consider ₹1,00,000 growing at an assumed 8% annually.

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: These are mathematical illustrations assuming steady annual growth, no tax, no costs and no withdrawals. Real investment returns are not smooth or guaranteed. The point is not the exact value. It is how strongly time influences compounding.
Compounding Needs Four Things
1. Time
More time gives growth more opportunities to build on itself.
2. Consistency
Regular contributions continue adding new capital.

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: 3. Suitable return
The investment must have a reasonable chance of supporting the goal after inflation, costs and tax. Higher expected returns usually involve greater uncertainty or risk.
4. Staying invested
Frequent withdrawals interrupt the process.
Starting Early Versus Investing More Later
Consider two simplified investors.
Investor A

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Investor B
Approximate results:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting
Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Both contribute the same total amount. Investor A’s money has more time to compound. These figures are illustrations. Actual returns vary, and investment costs and taxes are not included. The lesson is not that everyone must start with a large amount immediately. It is that waiting has a cost that may require much larger future contributions.
The Cost of Waiting

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Suppose a mother wants ₹50 lakh after 20 years. At an assumed return of 10%, she might need to invest approximately ₹6,600 a month. If she waits five years and has only 15 years remaining, the required monthly amount may rise to approximately ₹12,000. If she waits ten years and has only ten years remaining, it may rise to approximately ₹24,000. The exact result depends on the calculation method, timing and actual returns. But the pattern is clear:
**Less time requires more money or a smaller goal.**
Starting Now Does Not Mean Rushing

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: “Start early” does not mean:

items: Take risk you do not understand

blocks.text: A responsible start may simply mean:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: 1. Define the goal. 2. Estimate its future cost. 3. Choose a realistic timeline. 4. Understand available investment categories. 5. Begin with a sustainable amount. 6. Review and increase it when possible.
Compounding Can Work Against You Too
Debt can compound. If unpaid interest is added to the balance, future interest may be calculated on a larger amount. This is especially dangerous with:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: The same mathematical force that helps long-term investments can make expensive debt harder to escape. That is why reducing high-cost debt is often a priority.
Return Is Not the Only Variable
A future goal depends on:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Do not try to solve a future shortfall only by chasing a higher return. Higher-return investments may also bring greater volatility and loss risk. A safer response may combine:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Return Assumptions Should Be Modest
Financial projections are highly sensitive to return assumptions. Suppose ₹10,000 is invested monthly for 20 years.

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: These are mathematical illustrations using steady assumed returns. Actual investments do not grow in a straight line. A projection based on an unrealistic return may make an underfunded goal appear secure. Use assumptions as planning tools—not promises.

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Understand the Sequence of Returns

Two investments can have similar average returns but produce different experiences because gains and losses occur in different orders. This matters especially when money is being withdrawn, such as during retirement. A major market decline near the beginning of retirement can be more damaging than the same decline many years earlier, because withdrawals may force the investor to sell more units at lower values. This is called sequence-of-returns risk . It is one reason long-term planning needs:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: We will return to this in the retirement chapter.
Inflation and Children’s Education
Education planning should consider:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Do not build a plan around one course or institution when the child is very young. Use a reasonable goal range and update it periodically.
Inflation and Retirement

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Retirement may last several decades. Suppose a family needs ₹80,000 a month today. At 6% inflation, the equivalent monthly amount after 20 years would be approximately: ₹80,000 × (1.06)²⁰ ≈ ₹2,56,600 This does not mean every expense will rise exactly by 6%. It shows why retirement cannot be planned using today’s monthly cost alone.
Step-Up Investing
As income grows, a family may increase regular investment contributions. This is sometimes called a step-up strategy. For example:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: This represents a 10% annual increase. A step-up can help:

items: Prevent lifestyle inflation from consuming every raise

blocks.text: Do not promise an increase you cannot sustain. Review annually.
Lifestyle Inflation

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Lifestyle inflation occurs when spending rises automatically as income rises. Some improvement in lifestyle is natural and valuable. The problem begins when every increase in income is absorbed by:

items: Commitments that are difficult to reverse

blocks.text: A balanced income increase might support:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Decide before the money arrives.
A Simple Goal Calculation
Step 1 — Write today’s cost

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Step 2 — Choose a planning inflation rate
Assumed inflation: _______________%
Step 3 — Record the number of years
Time remaining: __________________ years
Step 4 — Estimate future cost
Use: Present cost × (1 + inflation)ⁿ Estimated future cost: ₹____________________
Step 5 — Record existing investments
Current amount set aside: ₹_________________

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Step 6 — Estimate required contribution
Use a reputable calculator or qualified planner with a reasonable return assumption. Required monthly contribution: ₹____________
Step 7 — Test the plan
Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

items: What if the return is lower?
What if the goal costs more?
Can I increase the amount later?
Is the investment suitable for the timeline?
When will I review the estimate?

blocks.text: Example: Updating a Goal
A family wants to fund a child’s education in 10 years.

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Estimated future cost: ₹15,00,000 × (1.08)¹⁰ ≈ ₹32,38,000 The family’s ₹4 lakh may grow over ten years, but it is unlikely to cover the entire estimated cost. The next step is not automatically to choose a more aggressive investment. The family can consider:

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Good planning presents choices instead of pretending the future is certain.
Use a Range, Not One Perfect Forecast
Rather than one assumption, create three scenarios.

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Do not build the family’s security solely on the optimistic scenario. If the plan works only when returns are excellent and inflation is low, it is fragile.

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Review, but Do Not React Constantly
Long-term plans should be reviewed:

items: After a large market movement, if allocation has shifted materially
After changes in tax or product rules

blocks.text: Do not change the plan after every headline. Frequent reaction can interrupt compounding and increase costs.

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.text: Common Mistakes

items: Planning future goals using today’s cost
Treating general inflation as identical to personal inflation
Keeping all long-term money in cash
Assuming investment growth will be smooth
Delaying because the first contribution feels too small
Trying to compensate for delay by taking excessive risk

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

items: Increasing lifestyle costs with every income increase
Confusing nominal return with real return
Failing to update education or retirement goals
Stopping contributions during ordinary market declines without reviewing the goal

pages.chapterTitle: Inflation, Compounding and the Cost of Waiting

blocks.label: RED FLAG: BE CAREFUL IF…
blocks.text: Someone promises a fixed high return for many years. Inflation is ignored in a long-term projection. A plan assumes unusually high returns without explaining risk. Fees and taxes are omitted from every illustration. An adviser presents a projection as a guarantee. You are urged to borrow money because investment returns will “definitely” exceed the loan cost. A product is recommended before the goal and timeline are discussed. You are encouraged to invest emergency money for better growth. The plan works only under its most optimistic assumptions. You cannot explain how the projected value was calculated.
